nodes.description: Anxiety and anxious behavior are common in DYRK1A syndrome, forming part of the behavioral/autistic phenotype alongside altered stress reactions.

evidence.snippet:
autism spectrum disorder 
including anxious and/or stereotypic behavior problems

evidence.reference_title: Ocular Phenotype Associated with DYRK1A Variants.

nodes.description: Autism spectrum disorder, frequently with anxious and/or stereotypic behavior problems, is a characteristic feature.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A Syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
autism spectrum disorder 
including anxious and/or stereotypic behavior problems

evidence.reference_title: Characterizing the autism spectrum phenotype in DYRK1A-related syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
Diagnosis of ASD was confirmed in 85% and ID was confirmed in 89% of 
participants with DYRK1A syndrome.

nodes.description: Congenital cardiac anomalies, including ventricular septal defect, patent ductus arteriosus, and aortic valve disease, are reported and warrant cardiac evaluation and follow-up.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A Syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
Ophthalmologic, urogenital, cardiac, and/or 
dental anomalies have been reported.

evidence.reference_title: Ocular Phenotype Associated with DYRK1A Variants.
evidence.snippet: cardiac features (including ventricular septal defect, patent ductus arteriosus, aortic valve disease)

nodes.description: Gastrointestinal problems, including constipation, are reported and require standard management.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A Syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
standard treatment for orthopedic, dental, cardiac, 
urogenital, ophthalmologic, constipation, and other medical issues.

nodes.description: DYRK1A-related intellectual disability syndrome is caused by heterozygous loss-of-function variants (truncating mutations, deletions, and disruptive missense changes) in DYRK1A, typically arising de novo. DYRK1A encodes a dosage-sensitive dual-specificity tyrosine-phosphorylation-regulated kinase that maps to the Down syndrome critical region on chromosome 21q22.13. Whereas copy-number gain (trisomy 21) increases DYRK1A dosage, loss-of-function variants produce haploinsufficiency, establishing DYRK1A as a dosage-sensitive gene in which both increased and decreased dosage impair neurodevelopment.

evidence.reference_title: Disruptive de novo mutations of DYRK1A lead to a syndromic form of autism and ID.
evidence.snippet:
Truncation of DYRK1A in patients with developmental delay (DD) and 
autism spectrum disorder (ASD) suggests a different pathology associated with 
loss-of-function mutations.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A Syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
DYRK1A syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder typically 
caused by a de novo pathogenic variant.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A haploinsufficiency causes a new recognizable syndrome with microcephaly, intellectual disability, speech impairment, and distinct facies.
evidence.snippet:
Dual-specificity tyrosine-(Y)-phosphorylation-regulated kinase 1 A (DYRK1A ) is 
a highly conserved gene located in the Down syndrome critical region. It has an 
important role in early development and regulation of neuronal proliferation.

nodes.description: Deep-set eyes are part of the recognizable facial gestalt of DYRK1A syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Ocular Phenotype Associated with DYRK1A Variants.
evidence.snippet: It has a particular facial gestalt of deep-set eyes, short nose with a broad tip, up-slanting palpebral fissures

nodes.description: DYRK1A regulates the cell cycle of cortical neural stem cells (radial glia) by controlling nuclear Cyclin D1 levels and G1-phase length, thereby balancing proliferative versus neurogenic divisions. Altered DYRK1A dosage disrupts the coupling of cell-cycle regulation and neuron production, producing a deficit in cortical projection neurons that underlies microcephaly and impaired corticogenesis. This dosage-sensitive control of neurogenesis explains how both DYRK1A loss-of-function (syndrome) and gain (Down syndrome) converge on cortical neuronal deficits.
nodes.id: Disrupted Cortical Neurogenesis and Cell-Cycle Control

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A-mediated Cyclin D1 Degradation in Neural Stem Cells Contributes to the Neurogenic Cortical Defects in Down Syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
the human DYRK1A 
kinase on chromosome 21 tightly regulates the nuclear levels of Cyclin D1 in 
embryonic cortical stem (radial glia) cells

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A-mediated Cyclin D1 Degradation in Neural Stem Cells Contributes to the Neurogenic Cortical Defects in Down Syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
These alterations promote asymmetric proliferative divisions at the 
expense of neurogenic divisions, producing a deficit in cortical projection 
neurons that persists in postnatal stages.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A roles in human neural progenitors.
evidence.snippet:
the overall impact is a marked reduction in 
hNSC proliferation.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A roles in human neural progenitors.
evidence.snippet:
We identified 35 protein partners of DYRK1A involved in essential 
pathways such as cell cycle regulation and DNA repair.

nodes.description: Febrile seizures of early onset in infancy are characteristic of DYRK1A syndrome and may precede the later development of afebrile epilepsy.

evidence.reference_title: Identification of two novel and one rare mutation in DYRK1A and prenatal diagnoses in three Chinese families with intellectual Disability-7.
evidence.snippet:
Intellectual disability-7 (MRD7) is a subtype disorder 
of intellectual disability (MRD) involving feeding difficulties, hypoactivity, 
and febrile seizures at an age of early onset

nodes.description: Feeding problems are commonly reported, contributing to growth restriction and requiring monitoring of nutritional status and safety of oral intake.

evidence.reference_title: Growth charts in DYRK1A syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
Low birth weight, growth 
restriction with feeding difficulties, stature insufficiency, and microcephaly 
are frequently reported.

nodes.description: Foot abnormalities, including small feet, toe syndactyly, and high arched feet, are part of the recognizable phenotype of DYRK1A syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A Syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
typical facial gestalt, feeding problems, seizures, hypertonia, gait 
disturbances, and foot anomalies.

evidence.reference_title: Ocular Phenotype Associated with DYRK1A Variants.
evidence.snippet: Hand and foot abnormalities include long tapered fingers, small hands and feet, toe syndactyly and high arched feet

nodes.description: Gait disturbances are commonly observed and contribute to the motor phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A Syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
a typical facial gestalt, feeding problems, seizures, hypertonia, gait 
disturbances, and foot anomalies.

nodes.description: Global psychomotor developmental delay is detected in infancy/early childhood and is a near-universal early feature of DYRK1A syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Growth charts in DYRK1A syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
Haploinsufficiency of DYRK1A causes a syndrome with 
global psychomotor delay and intellectual disability.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A haploinsufficiency causes a new recognizable syndrome with microcephaly, intellectual disability, speech impairment, and distinct facies.
evidence.snippet:
All 
individuals shared congenital microcephaly at birth, intellectual disability, 
developmental delay, severe speech impairment, short stature, and distinct 
facial features.

nodes.description: Hypertonia is part of the neurological phenotype, alongside gait disturbances.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A Syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
a typical facial gestalt, feeding problems, seizures, hypertonia, gait 
disturbances, and foot anomalies.

nodes.description: Beyond its early role in neural precursor proliferation, DYRK1A regulates the assembly of neocortical and hippocampal circuits. Reduced DYRK1A dosage alters the proportions of excitatory and inhibitory neurons and synapses, producing an excitatory/inhibitory (E/I) imbalance. At the circuit level, DYRK1A controls a hippocampal mossy fiber to parvalbumin interneuron feed-forward inhibition pathway required for social recognition. This circuit-level dysfunction provides a mechanistic link between DYRK1A haploinsufficiency and the autistic behavior, social-cognition deficits, and seizure susceptibility of the syndrome, acting downstream of and in parallel to the early neurogenic defect.
nodes.id: Impaired Neocortical Circuit Development and E/I Imbalance

evidence.reference_title: Impaired development of neocortical circuits contributes to the neurological alterations in DYRK1A haploinsufficiency syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
haploinsufficient Dyrk1a+/- mutant mice mirror the neurological traits 
associated with the human pathology, such as defective social interactions, 
stereotypic behaviors and epileptic activity.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired development of neocortical circuits contributes to the neurological alterations in DYRK1A haploinsufficiency syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
These mutant mice present altered 
proportions of excitatory and inhibitory neocortical neurons and synapses.

evidence.reference_title: An inhibitory circuit-based enhancer of DYRK1A function reverses Dyrk1a-associated impairment in social recognition.
evidence.snippet:
we identify a social 
experience-sensitive mechanism in hippocampal mossy fiber-parvalbumin 
interneuron (PV IN) synapses by which DYRK1A recruits feedforward inhibition of 
CA3 and CA2 to promote social recognition.

nodes.description: Severely impaired or absent speech development accompanies the intellectual disability in DYRK1A syndrome.

DYRK1A syndrome is characterized by intellectual

It was characterized by ID, ASD, microcephaly, 
intrauterine growth retardation, febrile seizures in infancy, impaired speech,

nodes.description: Reduced head circumference (often primary/congenital) is a hallmark and consistent feature of DYRK1A syndrome.

nodes.description: Stereotypic behaviors are very common in DYRK1A syndrome and form part of the behavioral/autistic phenotype.

nodes.description: Ocular abnormalities are a frequent and clinically actionable comorbidity in DYRK1A syndrome; in a combined cohort of 145 individuals, 62.1% had ocular manifestations, prompting recommendations for routine ophthalmology referral.

evidence.reference_title: Ocular Phenotype Associated with DYRK1A Variants.
evidence.snippet: Ninety out of 145 patients (62.1%) with heterozygous DYRK1A variants

nodes.description: Optic nerve hypoplasia is an important cause of visual impairment in DYRK1A syndrome; DYRK1A has an established role in optic nerve development, and Dyrk1a+/- mice show retinal ganglion cell and optic nerve axon deficits.

evidence.reference_title: Ocular Phenotype Associated with DYRK1A Variants.
evidence.snippet: optic nerve hypoplasia (13%, 12/90)

nodes.description: Refractive error (hyperopia/hypermetropia, myopia, astigmatism) is the most common ocular finding, present in 35.6% of DYRK1A individuals with ocular features; it is a treatable cause of visual impairment.

evidence.reference_title: Ocular Phenotype Associated with DYRK1A Variants.
evidence.snippet: refractive error (35.6%, 32/90)

nodes.description: Affected individuals develop febrile seizures in infancy and epilepsy with atonic, absence, and generalized myoclonic seizure types.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A Syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
the development of epilepsy with 
seizures of the atonic, absence, and generalized myoclonic types

nodes.description: Postnatal growth restriction with short stature/stature insufficiency is frequently observed, prompting the development of syndrome-specific growth charts.

evidence.reference_title: DYRK1A Syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
Other medical 
concerns relate to febrile seizures in infancy; the development of epilepsy with 
seizures of the atonic, absence, and generalized myoclonic types; short stature; 
and gastrointestinal problems.

nodes.description: Strabismus is a common ocular finding in DYRK1A syndrome, seen in 21.1% of individuals with ocular features, at an incidence well above the general population.

evidence.reference_title: Ocular Phenotype Associated with DYRK1A Variants.
evidence.snippet: strabismus (21.1%, 19/90)
